characters.physical_details: Average build, thinning hair at the crown, reading glasses, usually khakis and button-downs; owns one suit from the wedding and drives a gray Civic.
characters.occupation_status: Insurance adjuster at Mutual Fidelity in Columbus; handles residential property claims, especially water damage, and has worked there about 15 years.
characters.literacy_languages: English only; reads policies, contracts, and claims documentation closely.

state_transitions.change: Learns the wedding toast video is public and uncontrollable.
Signs an exclusive management contract with Layla and Banks Management.
Starts regular writing sessions with Simone.
Learns the toast's effect is not repeatable as a prepared keynote.
Learns Kelsey found his divorce spreadsheet at sixteen and has carried that knowledge for twelve years.
Learns unscripted travel material works better than prepared club bits.
Sets aside the 17-page script and tapes 63 unscripted minutes at the Lincoln Theatre.
Reopens the second bedroom and leaves the door ajar instead of shut.

characters.physical_details: Dark hair, direct gaze, practical clothes outside work, usually in dental-practice scrubs on the job.
characters.occupation_status: Dental hygienist at a Cleveland practice.
characters.availability: Present by text in chapter_01; in person in chapters 5, 13, and 17; text follow-up in chapter_18; lives in Cleveland with Tyler.

characters.physical_details: Dark-haired, well-kept, deliberate in dress and manner.
characters.occupation_status: Works in nonprofit administration and fundraising in Columbus.
characters.availability: Present by text or phone in chapters 1 and 5; in person in chapters 8 and 17; referenced in chapter_18; lives with Brendan in Upper Arlington.

characters.physical_details: Tall, fit, graying at the temples, medically competent-looking, steady-handed.
characters.literacy_languages: English; medically and generally well read.
characters.availability: Present in chapters 5 (via Megan), 8, and 17; referenced in chapter_18; lives with Megan in Upper Arlington.

characters.physical_details: Small, kinetic, leather-jacket-and-boots energy, nearly always checking her phone.
characters.occupation_status: Runs Banks Management, a 12-client New York talent-management company.

state_transitions.change: Signs Doug as an exclusive management client through Banks Management.

characters.availability: Present in chapters 4, 6, 16, and 17; referenced or on phone/text in chapters 12 and 18.

characters.physical_details: Plain office clothes, paper-file habits, dented 2011 Camry, unshowy steady presence.
characters.occupation_status: Claims adjuster at Mutual Fidelity; lives in Gahanna.
characters.literacy_languages: English; newspaper reader and regular library borrower.
characters.availability: Present in chapters 1-3, 9 (phone), 12, 16 (phone), and 18; absent from the Lincoln Theatre taping.

characters.physical_details: Mid-forties, expensive sneakers, restless notebook-and-phone development-exec energy.
Practical clothes, hair clipped up, notebook and coffee always nearby, no performance styling.
characters.occupation_status: Freelance comedy writer based in New York; formerly did stand-up for eleven years, plus improv and sketch, before quitting performance work.

state_transitions.change: Identifies Doug's usable voice in exact, undesigned observation and begins shaping material around it.

characters.availability: Present in chapters 7, 11-17; in Columbus for the development and taping stretch.

characters.physical_details: Small, sharp-featured, short white hair, blue cardigans and spotless white sneakers; physically slower but exact.
characters.occupation_status: Retired; lives at Maple Glen Senior Living near Dayton.
characters.literacy_languages: English; reads, watches local news, and takes notes.
characters.availability: Present in chapters 10 and 17; phones in chapter_18.
Present at the wedding; mentioned in chapters 5 and 13; attends the taping in chapter_17; referenced in chapter_18.

characters.physical_details: Young, earnest, denim-jacketed, recorder-and-notebook reporter with constant spring allergies.

timeline.story_start: Sunday morning in February, the day after Kelsey and Tyler's wedding reception and Doug's viral toast.
timeline.estimated_span: About four months, from February through June.

seasonal_track.season: February: late-winter Columbus; cold, gray, heaters still running.
March into April: LA is warm and bright; Ohio is thawing but still cool.
May: pollen-heavy, fully green late spring; the three-city tour happens at the end of the month.
Early June: warm evenings; rehearsal, taping, premiere, and return to work.

key_events.event: Kelsey and Tyler's wedding reception and Doug's toast
key_events.timing: Saturday night before chapter_01; Marriott ballroom in Worthington; about 180 guests
Sunday and Monday after the wedding; 14 million views by Monday morning

key_events.event: Simone starts regular sessions with Doug
Dinner with Megan and Brendan in Upper Arlington
Astelline Pharmaceuticals keynote fails in Indianapolis
Doug visits Gloria at Maple Glen
Kelsey reveals she found Doug's spreadsheet at sixteen
Cleveland, Pittsburgh, and Detroit warm-up sets

key_events.timing: Early June, two days before taping

key_events.event: Lincoln Theatre taping; Doug abandons the script and talks for sixty-three minutes
The special premieres and Doug returns to Mutual Fidelity
key_events.timing: Following weekend and Monday in June

geography.primary_setting: Columbus, Ohio, especially Clintonville, Worthington, Upper Arlington, the Short North, and East Long Street; key trips extend to Dayton, Cleveland, Indianapolis, Pittsburgh, Detroit, and Los Angeles.

key_locations.name: Doug's apartment
key_locations.details: Two-bedroom Clintonville apartment with a leaning Ikea bookshelf, one art print, a counter where he eats dinner standing up, a glass shower, and a second bedroom full of unopened boxes.

key_locations.name: Mutual Fidelity office
key_locations.details: Second-floor Sawyer Road office with about thirty desks in a beige open plan; Doug and Roy work side by side.

key_locations.name: Sal's sandwich place
key_locations.details: Doug and Roy's unchanged lunch routine for nine years; booth by the window; Roy always orders turkey on wheat with mustard and no tomato.

key_locations.name: Megan and Brendan's house
key_locations.details: Upper Arlington house with a large working kitchen, dining room, and clear signs of more money and more maintenance than Doug's apartment.

key_locations.name: Maple Glen Senior Living
key_locations.details: One-story retirement complex off Route 35 near Dayton; Gloria's room and green-carpet dining room are important recurring spaces.

key_locations.name: Lincoln Theatre
key_locations.details: East Long Street theater with a 580-seat contract capacity, balcony, vaudeville-era interior, backstage green room, and ghost light.

key_locations.name: The Thai restaurant
key_locations.details: Columbus restaurant where Doug and Kelsey have an established father-daughter routine; she always orders pad thai and he orders massaman curry.

distances: Columbus to Dayton/Maple Glen: about 70 miles, roughly 1 hour
Columbus to Cleveland: about 2 hours by car
Columbus to Indianapolis: about 2.5 hours by car
Cleveland to Pittsburgh: about 2.5 hours by car
Pittsburgh to Detroit: about 4.5 hours by car
Columbus to Los Angeles: cross-country flight

world_rules: Contemporary realistic fiction; no speculative elements or altered social rules.
Internet virality behaves in recognizable real-world ways: reposts, memes, thinkpieces, talk-show interest, streaming metrics, and public misreading.
Insurance logic matters: deductibles, endorsements, actual cash value, replacement cost, liability, depreciation, and documentation are all used accurately and shape Doug's thinking.
The toast's force cannot be recreated by ordinary set construction; scripting and formatting flatten it, while unneatened observation can briefly recover it.

power_structure.over: Doug's bookings, negotiations, and public opportunities
The special's framing, timetable, and distribution

power_structure.mechanism: She decides what is alive versus dead on the page and on stage
Viral circulation, commentary, and the reduction of a private event into content
Unsoftened maternal judgment he cannot ignore
She alone can reveal or withhold what she knows about the spreadsheet and the toast's impact

objects.name: The toast video
objects.origin: Phone upload from a wedding guest account called KelseyAndTylerWedding
objects.status: Viral artifact with about 80 million views by spring; source of memes, profiles, and the special.

objects.name: Doug's suit
objects.origin: His one suit, worn to the wedding
objects.status: Primary relic of the toast night; not used for the taping.

objects.name: The management contract
objects.origin: Eleven-page contract signed in Doug's apartment
objects.status: Active; 2-year term with 60 days' written notice; 20% entertainment/media, 15% publishing, 25% endorsements; pre-existing salary excluded; mutual indemnification; 30-month tail; New York law.

objects.name: The spreadsheet
objects.origin: Excel file left open on Doug's Indianola laptop during the divorce
objects.status: Tracks Megan's missed pickups, permission-slip lapses, and schedule conflicts in rows marked for documentation; Kelsey found it at sixteen and read herself as evidence.

objects.name: The boxes in the second bedroom
objects.origin: Packed when he moved out of the Briarwood house after the divorce
objects.status: Seven labeled boxes remain unopened for twelve years, including KELSEY, PHOTOS/ALBUMS, and BOOKS (2 OF 3); by the end the bedroom door is left partly open.

objects.name: The Briarwood kitchen table
objects.owner: Subsequent owners of the Briarwood house
objects.origin: Maple table from Doug and Megan's former kitchen on Briarwood.
objects.status: Still in the sold house; one leg was gouged on delivery and Kelsey scratched the top with a protractor; Doug regrets not taking it in the divorce.

objects.name: The Burris/Commonwealth claim file
objects.origin: Westerville pool-wall failure that flooded Margaret Commonwealth's rose garden.
objects.status: Recurring story Doug uses as material; canonical file version is a February failure that kills 17 hybrid tea roses valued at $4,218.47, later partly recovered through an overlooked $1,600 umbrella-policy payout.

objects.name: Doug's Netflix special
objects.origin: Lincoln Theatre taping after Doug abandons the prepared 17-page script.
objects.status: 63-minute unscripted special; 1.4 million households start it in the first 48 hours, and reactions split between boring and devastating.

objects.name: Megan's shampoo
objects.origin: The shower-ledged bottle whose absence made the separation feel real to Doug
objects.status: Exists only in memory and in the story Doug finally tells Simone.

objects.name: The art print
objects.origin: Bought by Megan at a Columbus gallery and left behind because it did not match the new house

income_sources.amount: Unspecified fees for the Cleveland, Pittsburgh, and Detroit sets
Exact fee not stated in the text; large enough that Gloria asks for the number and approves the result

knowledge_state.knows: The toast publicly exposed real feelings about his marriage, fatherhood, and loneliness and cannot be taken back.
knowledge_state.hidden_from: No one; the toast is public

knowledge_state.knows: Doug kept a spreadsheet tracking Megan's co-parenting failures and schedule lapses involving Kelsey; she found it at sixteen.
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Doug until chapter_13; Megan possibly indefinitely

knowledge_state.knows: Kelsey found Doug's spreadsheet and has carried that knowledge into her marriage.
knowledge_state.learned_in: Pre-novel (last year via Kelsey; disclosed in chapter_13)

knowledge_state.knows: Kelsey found his divorce spreadsheet at sixteen and experienced herself as evidence inside it.
Doug's material works when he stops translating himself and speaks in exact, undesigned observation.
Prepared structure flattens him and unscripted truth is the only version that reliably works onstage.
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Layla and Charlie keep trying to format the act anyway

environmental_constants: Doug's Clintonville apartment stays small and exact: leaning Ikea shelf, single art print, counter dinners, glass shower, unopened second-bedroom boxes.
Mutual Fidelity stays a beige thirty-desk claims floor under fluorescent lights, built around documentation and other people's household disasters.
Sal's and Roy's fixed lunch order anchor the workweek.
Maple Glen remains institutional but lived-in: green carpet, round tables, plastic flowers, and retirement-community food.
The Lincoln Theatre is an echoing old vaudeville house with red seats, balcony sightlines, backstage cables, and a ghost light.
Seasonal movement matters: gray February, thawing April, pollen-heavy May, and warm June evenings.
